comments.author: @sabelonxumalo1937
comments.text: Great presentation for a white man. As a liSwati living in Eswatini, I feel he did very well. The pronunciation of names sucks, but i forgive him; he is not African. His presentation of our traditional events is very much like Umhlanga is like that. The real Events will blow your mind. It's not 40 000 but 100 000 girls dancing with pride to be part of their Royal Family. They are not forced, as some Western media say. I wish he could use a local editor to show traditional events as they follow each other. Great work.

comments.author: @danepikkola9119
comments.text: From 1992 to '94 I was with the Peace Corps teaching at Masaphula high school on the southern border. Much here is accurate, but the AI narration is a real turn off. Much of the pronunciation is not correct. It is true that Eswatini (will always be Swaziland for me) is unique in Africa, and I highly recommend anyone visit. Don't get me started on the monarchy, however. It was life changing to live there amongst regular people on my homestead in Makhosisi, and I am eternally grateful for my experience with my adopted family. There is so much more to tell.  Ngiyabonga Eswatini. Vusi Mhlanga.

comments.author: @UHURU-001
comments.text: Eswatini (or the landlocked kingdom of Swaziland) is one of my favourite places on Earth.  I spent months exploring those mountains in the west, bordering the Barberton mountainlands and the Kruger National Park in South Africa, where some of the oldest rocks on the planet are found.   Another favourite place for me is Lesotho (or the landlocked kingdom of Basutoland) and the magnificent Drakensberg Mountains on the border with Kwa Zulu Natal, South Africa, A UNESCO World Heritage reserve.
